fn1: Victory interpreted as winning Droupadi’s hand.
fn5: Agallochum, a soft, resinous wood with an aromatic smell burnt as perfume.
fn6: That is, those of the lunar dynasty, an epithet for the Panchalas.
fn7: Kandarpa, one of the names of the god of love.
fn14: Halayudha is one of Balarama’s names. Hala means plough, ayudha means weapon. Halayudha is one with the plough as one’s weapon.
fn19: Therefore, they did not notice the Pandavas.
fn20: Pritha is Kunti’s name and only Yudhishthira, Bhima and Arjuna were Kunti’s sons. Nakula and Sahadeva were Madri’s sons.
fn22: The science of archery. In general, the knowledge of arms.
fn23: Varnas; the Shudras are not mentioned here.
fn25: Bhima and Arjuna, who had not left the assembly. Arjuna had only left the arena.
fn28: Usually a name used for Krishna, but here used for Balarama.
fn29: Karaka, more speciﬁcally, water pots made out of coconut shells.
fn32: In the shower of arrows, because they were swiftly shooting arrows at each other.
fn39: Purandara is Indra’s name. Hence, Indra’s weapon.
fn42: It is not very clear who this Brahmana leading the way is, and must be a reference to Dhoumya.
fn48: Meaning, the south. This is a reference to the sage Agastya’s journey to the south.
fn50: The own varna means a Kshatriya. The higher varna means a Brahmana.
fn51: The word used is vama, which means left, as well as lower being. In this context, lower being seems best.
fn52: The word ‘sons’ is used here in the sense of descendants.
fn53: The daityas. Although the word Indra isn’t directly used, the suggestion is clearly that he resembled Indra.
fn55: Yudhishthira, Nakula and Sahadeva, who had returned earlier.
fn60: Tribe, family or race, for want of a better word.
